text.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Multi-Agent Orchestration Decision Tree application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Default to single-agent + tools. Multi-agent justified only when: tool count > 30, distinct domains required, or judge-actor split provides verifiable quality gain.
rule.rationale: Single agent + tools is simpler + cheaper + easier to debug; multi-agent overhead must be earned.

rule.statement: Hierarchical (planner → workers): use when tasks have clear ordering + bounded subtasks. Collaborative (peer agents): use for open-ended exploration + multi-perspective.
rule.rationale: Mismatched topology costs 30-100% extra latency + cost; correct topology is the dominant variable.

rule.statement: Hand-off carries: task ID, scoped context (not full conversation), success criteria, escalation path. Stream-JSON is fine for low-stakes; for high-stakes, use IDs + structured payload.
rule.rationale: Hand-off design is where multi-agent systems break; explicit protocol reduces context bleed + failure modes.

rule.statement: Separate judge agent from actor only when judge improves quality > judge cost. Measure with eval; cargo-cult judges waste 30-50% of budget.
rule.rationale: Judge-actor split is fashionable; empirical evidence often shows zero quality lift at substantial cost.

rule.statement: Track: latency, cost, quality vs single-agent baseline. If multi-agent loses on 2 of 3, roll back. Re-evaluate quarterly.
rule.rationale: Multi-agent locks in cost + complexity; explicit rollback trigger prevents lock-in to wrong choice.
